sanskrit_devanagari:
अथ चैनं नित्यजातं नित्यं वा मन्यसे मृतम् ।
तथापि त्वं महाबाहो नैनं शोचितुमर्हसि ॥ २६ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
atha cainaṁ nitya-jātaṁ nityaṁ vā manyase mṛtam |
tathāpi tvaṁ mahā-bāho nainaṁ śocitum arhasi || 26 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical; line-break inserted between hemistichs in source pack)
exact (IAST text body identical to vedabase; hyphenation differs)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.026 = MBh_06,024.026). Body text identical — 'nainaṃ' in second pada confirmed.

anvaya.meaning: if, however / on the other hand

translations.verbatim_quote: If, however, you think that the soul [or the symptoms of life] will always be born and die forever, you still have no reason to lament, O mighty-armed.
If, however, you think that the self is subject to constant birth and death, O mighty-armed Arjun, even then you should not grieve like this.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: There is always a class of philosophers, almost akin to the Buddhists, who do not believe in the separate existence of the soul beyond the body. When Lord Kṛṣṇa spoke the Bhagavad-gītā, it appears that such philosophers existed, and they were known as the Lokāyatikas and Vaibhāṣikas.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.26 This (verse), 'On the other hand,' etc., is uttered assuming that the Self is transient. Atha ca, on the other hand, if (— conveys the sense of assumption —); following ordinary experience, manyase, you think; enam, this One, the Self under discussion; is nityajatam, born continually.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads 2.26 as a concessive argument: even if one rejects the imperishable doctrine of the previous verses and adopts the ordinary-experience view that the Self is born repeatedly and dies repeatedly with each body, the conclusion still holds — Arjuna should not grieve. The verse is structured as 'on the other hand' (atha ca) marking a hypothesis Krishna does not himself endorse but uses to close every escape route. The 'iva'-style assumption: take the opponent's premise; the conclusion is unchanged.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.26 Besides, if you consider this self as identical with the body, which is constantly born and constantly dies — which is nothing other than these characteristics of the body mentioned above —, even then it does not become you to feel grief; because, birth and death are inevitable for the body.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja takes the conditional concretely: if you identify the Self with the body, then the body's nature is precisely birth-and-death. Grief over the body's death is grief over the body being what it is. The conditional collapses on itself — the very view that justifies grief makes grief incoherent. Even on the materialist premise, the right conclusion is non-grief.

positions.rendering: on the other hand (conveys the sense of assumption — Krishna is granting a premise he does not himself hold)
besides, if you consider — taking the materialist position as a hypothesis

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All translators agree atha ca opens a conditional, but the depth of the conditional matters. Shankara's 'assumption' reading is strong: the verse explicitly entertains a doctrine Krishna has just spent six verses refuting. The English 'if, however' is a flat rendering; the Sanskrit signals 'taking the opposite view for argument's sake.' Ramanuja sharpens this into 'identifying Self with body,' which makes the concessive collapse on itself — birth-and-death is the body's definition, so grieving the body's death is grieving the body for being a body.

literal_meaning: And even if you think this [Self] is constantly born and constantly dies, even then, O mighty-armed, you ought not grieve for this [Self].
traditional_meaning_consensus: A concessive argument. Krishna closes the escape route: even if one rejects the previous verses' doctrine of the imperishable Self and adopts the materialist or Buddhist view that the Self is constantly born and constantly dies along with each body, grief is still unjustified. Why grief is unjustified on this alternate premise is given in the next verse (2.27). The commentarial tradition (Shankara, Ramanuja, Sridhara, Keshava, Abhinavagupta) is unanimous that 2.26 is hypothetical — Krishna is not endorsing the materialist view, only granting it for argument's sake to demonstrate that the conclusion holds either way. The verse begins the second leg of the duty argument: even on the opposing premise, grief fails.
